topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The discovery hit Liam like a physical blow. He found the laptop open on Maya’s desk, the screen glowing with a manuscript. His stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just beneath his ribs. It was a novel. And the protagonist, every single agonizingly detailed scene, was him. He stared at the words—*“The morning light caught the familiar line of his jaw, a silhouette I’d come to memorize.”*—and a flush crept up his neck. He felt a sudden, hot surge of disbelief, quickly followed by a sharp, brittle anger that made his jaw lock. When Maya finally looked up, her expression was soft, almost apologetic. "It's just... a creative exercise, Liam. I was exploring themes." Liam didn't speak for a long moment, just gripping the edge of the desk until his knuckles were white. He slowly closed the laptop, the click echoing too loudly in the quiet room.

[story 2]

Sarah walked into the living room and stopped dead, her breath catching in her throat. Tucked beneath a stack of books was a heavily annotated manuscript, clearly a work of fiction, and every chapter seemed to revolve around her life—her habits, her favorite coffee order, the precise way she laughed. A low, simmering heat began to build in Sarah’s chest, tightening her shoulders. She picked up the nearest page, her fingers trembling slightly as she read a passage describing a shared evening, written with an intimacy that felt utterly misplaced. "What in the world is this?" she managed, her voice dangerously level. When Mark entered, beaming, Sarah didn't look up. She just pointed a rigid finger at the screen. "This," she stated, her tone clipped and devoid of warmth, "is inappropriate." Mark’s smile faltered instantly, replaced by a look of startled confusion.

[story 3]

Elias found the evidence tucked inside a seldom-used drawer: a thick, leather-bound notebook filled with prose. He flipped through the pages, and the narrative was undeniably his—the subtle nuances of his anxieties, the way he habitually chewed his lip when concentrating. A profound sense of offense washed over him, settling deep in his core. He felt a sudden, sharp need to push everything away. When Chloe walked in, carrying groceries, Elias didn't bother to hide the notebook. He held it up, his gaze fixed on her with an unyielding severity. "This is mine," he articulated, his voice strained. "You shouldn't have been writing about me like this." Chloe froze mid-step, her expression shifting from casual to utterly bewildered. The air in the hallway seemed to drop several degrees.
